text: The present article is an analysis of the Greek version of the last pericope of the Book of the Wisdom of Sirach, namely Sir 51:13-30. The entire text can be divided into two parts. In the first part (vv. 13-22) the author relates his own experience of the search for wisdom and its being found. In the second part (vv. 23-30) he exhorts the uneducated to come to his school and share in the wisdom he has found. The wisdom for which the sage asks and which he has found is of a religious nature (he asked for it in prayer – vv. 13b, 14a, 19c; it manifests itself in good deeds – v. 19b). In the first part of the pericope it is clearly stated that the basic condition for finding wisdom is that one begins to seek it from an early age (vv. 13a, 15d). It is also explained how wisdom can be found (a human being should subordinate all spheres of life to finding it – vv. 13b, 14b, 15b, 19a, 21a). The means of finding wisdom are also listed (purification – v. 20b; following the path of righteousness – v. Sir 51:13-30: An Analysis of the Greek Version The means of finding wisdom are also listed (purification – v. 20b; following the path of righteousness – v. 15c; striving for goodness – v. 18a; turning to wisdom – v. 20a; complying with the Law – v. 19b; listening – v. 16a; repentance for the mistakes made – v. 19d). In the pericope it is also mentioned what kind of gifts wisdom brings to those who have found it. The gifts of wisdom are joy (v. 15b), formation (v. 16b), heart (intellectual capabilities) (v. 20c) and language (through which it is possible to praise God and share wisdom with others) (v. 22a). The whole set of these gifts is called a good possession (v. 21b). The finding of wisdom is not depicted as being particularly difficult to achieve, nor as demanding great effort (v. 16a). What is important is that the seeking of wisdom should be a permanent feature of the life of those who want to have wisdom (they should never stop searching for it – v. 14b). In the second part of the pericope (vv. 23-30) of clearly didactic character, the author exhorts the uneducated to come to his school so that he could share wisdom with them. The author exhorts those who, like himself, desire wisdom to make use of his experience and to undergo the formation offered in his school. The Book of the Wisdom of Sirach presents two ways which lead to wisdom. The first way involves the personal participation of the individual who seeks wisdom, while the second way is that of taking possession of wisdom which has already been found and which is communicated by someone else. The author of the Book of the Wisdom of Sirach has clearly optimistic views on the possibility of finding and having wisdom. His attitude is different from that of the author of the Book of Job and the Book of Ecclesiastes. The present pericope may be seen as a summary of the author’s optimism as it is expounded in the Book of the Wisdom of Sirach. The author mentions his experience in searching for and finding wisdom in Sir 6:18-37; 14:20 – 15:10; 24, 33:18-19. Together with the prologue (Sir 1:1-10) it also frames the whole book and constitutes the summary of the most important theological ideas of the Book of the Wisdom of Sirach and at the same time a synthesis of the author’s theology.
